One growing corpus feeds a ladder of models trained on nested fractions of it. Because every fraction is the same quality, smaller corpora are prefixes of larger ones, so no data is regenerated per rung, and models are built while generation is still running so the expensive step can be stopped early.

A single config (configs/scale/world.yaml) defines the full generation target and largest model plus a scale section of rungs, each a fraction, a model preset, and per-rung training overrides. The Slurm submitter runs generation in cumulative chunks (using the resumable generator to top up to each rung's target); when a chunk is merged into a frozen snapshot the corresponding rung trains pretrain, finetune, and evaluate on that snapshot while the next chunk keeps generating. Each rung reads its snapshot through project.corpus_dir and writes its own tokenizer, packed data, checkpoints, and reports under its own out_dir.

This replaces the earlier ladder of three self-contained scale configs (s0_pico, s1_nano, s2_micro), each of which regenerated its own corpus. It rests on the observation that referent-relaxed, multi-domain generation produces uniform quality across the corpus, so a fraction is a representative, nested subset (each cumulative chunk adds a type-balanced increment, so a snapshot at fraction f holds about f of each text type). The motivation is that data generation is the most expensive step: building models progressively as fractions land lets a problem be caught early and the whole run cancelled with scancel, rather than paying for full generation before training reveals a fault. It also dovetails with the decision to keep and improve the finetune stage (node 15, node 30), because every rung trains and evaluates finetune, yielding a report_sft at every scale on each run as cheap, repeated signal. It reads on the generate stage (node 11) and the from-scratch training and evaluation stages (node 14, node 15, node 16), and is orthogonal to the eventual world-state generator (node 36), which concerns how the corpus is authored rather than how models are scheduled across fractions of it.
